content.text: Fitness Functions for Token Cost Guardrails

Token cost is an operational risk unique to GenAI applications. A bug that causes runaway LLM calls, an oversized max_tokens value, or a batch job without a concurrency cap can multiply costs by 10× before anyone notices. Unlike CPU or memory costs that scale predictably with traffic, token costs scale with both traffic and input size — a single large document sent to the model can cost as much as hundreds of small requests.

This document defines fitness functions that enforce token cost guardrails at four layers: static analysis at CI time, adapter-level request guardrails, aggregate monitoring checks, and periodic audit fitness functions. Together, these layers prevent cost surprises at every stage of the development and operations lifecycle.

attrs.alt: Four layers of token cost guardrails from static CI checks to periodic audits

content.text: Why Token Cost Requires Fitness Functions

Standard cost controls (cloud billing alerts, budget caps) operate with a lag of minutes to hours and act after money has been spent. Fitness functions shift cost governance left — they catch misconfiguration before code is merged, not after it runs in production.
max_tokens not set (defaults to model max)
Batch job without concurrency cap causes 100× traffic spike
First batch run in staging or production
Input document is 50k tokens — exceeds budget
stop_reason == max_tokens generates incomplete output that is retried indefinitely
Feature with low traffic suddenly goes viral — cost spikes 20× in one hour
Prompt template grew from 800 to 3000 tokens over six months (gradual bloat)
New model version costs 3× more than previous — team didn't check pricing

Every dollar of LLM cost that is caught at CI time costs nothing to fix. Every dollar caught at production alerting has already been spent. Fitness functions are cost-free insurance against the most common token cost failure modes.
Static fitness functions run at CI time, require no API calls, and complete in seconds. They check the source code and configuration for patterns that are known to cause cost overruns.
FF-COST-01: max_tokens Set on Every LLM Call

is not set, the Anthropic API uses the model's maximum context window. For claude-sonnet-4-6, the default is 8192 output tokens — roughly 6,000 words. A single call that hits the default instead of a task-appropriate limit (512 for summarisation, 2048 for generation) can cost 4–16× more than intended.

A batch job that calls the LLM once per item in an unbounded list can exhaust the API rate limit and run up unexpected costs. Every batch LLM loop must have a concurrency cap (semaphore or thread pool) and, if the batch can be large, a per-run item limit.
FF-COST-03: Token Budget Documented in Prompt YAML
Every prompt YAML must declare its expected token budget in the
field. This makes the cost contract explicit and enables the weekly audit to detect budget drift.

# tests/architecture/cost/test_prompt_token_budget.py
import yaml, pathlib, pytest

PROMPTS_DIR = pathlib.Path("prompts/")

@pytest.mark.parametrize("prompt_file", list(PROMPTS_DIR.glob("**/*.yaml")))
def test_prompt_has_token_budget(prompt_file):
    data = yaml.safe_load(prompt_file.read_text())

    assert "token_budget" in data, (
        f"FF-COST-03 FAILED: {prompt_file} missing 'token_budget' field.\n"
        "Add a token_budget section to document the expected cost:\n"
        "  token_budget:\n"
        "    max_input_tokens: 2000   # typical system + user message\n"
        "    max_output_tokens: 512   # must match max_tokens field\n"
        "    p95_cost_usd: 0.002      # calculated from model pricing\n"
        "    monthly_budget_usd: 500  # approved by EM"
    )

    budget = data["token_budget"]
    required_keys = {"max_input_tokens", "max_output_tokens", "p95_cost_usd"}
    missing = required_keys - set(budget.keys())
    assert not missing, (
        f"FF-COST-03 FAILED: {prompt_file} token_budget missing keys: {missing}"
    )

    # max_output_tokens in budget must match max_tokens at top level
    if "max_tokens" in data:
        assert data["max_tokens"] == budget["max_output_tokens"], (
            f"FF-COST-03 FAILED: {prompt_file} max_tokens ({data['max_tokens']}) "
            f"does not match token_budget.max_output_tokens ({budget['max_output_tokens']}). "
            "These must be equal."
        )
FF-COST-04: Model Upgrade Requires Budget Review
Upgrading from a cheaper model (Haiku) to a more capable but expensive one (Sonnet, Opus) should not happen silently. Any PR that changes the
field in a prompt YAML to a higher pricing tier must also update the
field to reflect the new pricing.
Layer 2 guardrails run inside the LLM adapter on every request. They cannot be skipped by calling code and provide a safety net independent of whether the caller correctly set token limits.

If the input message (system + user combined) exceeds the configured token budget, the adapter truncates the user message to fit rather than letting the API return a context-length error or silently truncate the output.
The adapter emits a cost metric on every call using the token counts from the API response and the model's per-token price. This is the source of truth for the Layer 3 aggregate alerts.

# src/adapters/llm_adapter.py (cost metric section)
from opentelemetry import metrics

# Anthropic pricing (USD per million tokens) — update when pricing changes
PRICING = {
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001":  {"input": 0.80,  "output": 4.00},
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":          {"input": 3.00,  "output": 15.00},
    "claude-opus-4-8":            {"input": 15.00, "output": 75.00},
}

_meter      = metrics.get_meter("llm.adapter")
_cost_gauge = _meter.create_histogram(
    "llm.cost_usd",
    unit="USD",
    description="Cost of a single LLM API call in USD",
)
_token_gauge = _meter.create_histogram(
    "llm.tokens",
    unit="tokens",
    description="Token usage per LLM call (input + output)",
)

def _emit_cost_metric(self, response, feature: str):
    model   = response.model or self.model
    pricing = PRICING.get(model, {"input": 0, "output": 0})
    input_tokens  = response.usage.input_tokens
    output_tokens = response.usage.output_tokens
    cost = (input_tokens * pricing["input"] + output_tokens * pricing["output"]) / 1_000_000

    attrs = {"llm.model": model, "llm.feature": feature}
    _cost_gauge.record(cost, attrs)
    _token_gauge.record(input_tokens,  {**attrs, "llm.token_type": "input"})
    _token_gauge.record(output_tokens, {**attrs, "llm.token_type": "output"})

Layer 3 checks run in near-real-time against aggregated metrics. They detect cost anomalies that are invisible at the per-call level — a gradual increase in average token count, a feature that started making twice as many calls, or a cost spike from increased traffic.
Layer 4 runs weekly (or before each major release) and catches cost problems that are invisible to real-time monitoring: gradual prompt bloat, model version drift, and budget vs actual variance.
Every week, compare the current prompt template sizes against the sizes from four weeks ago. A prompt that has grown by more than 20% in four weeks is likely accumulating unnecessary instructions.

# .github/workflows/weekly-cost-audit.yml
name: Weekly Token Cost Audit

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 8 * * 1'    # Every Monday at 08:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:         # Allow manual trigger

jobs:
  cost-audit:
    name: "Layer 4 — Weekly Cost Audit"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install pyyaml requests

      - name: "FF-COST-AUDIT-01: Prompt bloat check"
        run: python scripts/audit_prompt_size.py

      - name: "FF-COST-AUDIT-02: Actual vs budgeted cost"
        run: python scripts/audit_cost_vs_budget.py
        env:
          DD_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.DD_API_KEY }}
          DD_APP_KEY: ${{ secrets.DD_APP_KEY }}

      - name: Send Slack summary
        if: always()
        run: python scripts/send_cost_audit_slack.py
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.COST_AUDIT_SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
Add pre-commit hook that runs max_tokens check before git commit
Block prompt PR if token_budget field absent
If higher: investigate whether batch jobs have correct concurrency caps
If overrun: EM reviews and adjusts budget or optimises prompt within sprint
Require PR to remove equal or more tokens than added
Token cost is an operational risk unique to GenAI: bugs, oversized parameters, and unbounded batch jobs can multiply costs 10–100× before monitoring alerts fire
Four guardrail layers defend against cost overruns at different points in the lifecycle: CI static checks, adapter request guardrails, aggregate monitoring, and weekly audits
Layer 1 fitness functions (static analysis) catch the highest-impact cost risks — missing max_tokens and unbounded batch loops — before code merges, when fixes cost nothing
Layer 2 adapter guardrails (input pre-truncation, stop_reason check, cost metric emission) provide a runtime safety net that cannot be bypassed by calling code
Layer 3 monitoring alerts catch real-time cost spikes and traffic anomalies with a 1–5 minute lag; they should trigger investigation, not panic
Layer 4 weekly audits detect slow-moving cost problems — prompt bloat, model version drift, and budget vs actual variance — that real-time monitoring misses
